meta.title: Projectile Motion - The Archer's Arrow
meta.source: Classical mechanics - projectile motion with component analysis
meta.answer: The arrow travels approximately 141.4 meters before hitting the ground.

problem.text: An archer shoots an arrow at 30° above horizontal with initial speed 40 m/s. Find how far the arrow travels before hitting the ground.
problem.highlight: Find the horizontal range of a projectile launched at 30° with v₀ = 40 m/s

viz_requirements.description: Interactive 2D archery range with coordinate plane, showing projectile motion with real-time component vectors, trajectory path, and synchronized motion graphs

actions.description: Draw archer figure at origin with bow
Display initial velocity vector from bow
Animate velocity vector splitting into components
Start arrow projectile motion with physics
Update component vector sizes based on time
Dim other components to focus on one
Draw attention to object with pulse
Complete motion replay with all components
Overlay football field or other scale reference

nodes.title: The Archer's Challenge
nodes.objective: Establish the problem and build intuition for projectile motion complexity
nodes.context_from_previous: Starting fresh - no prior context needed

beat_outline.narration_hint: An archer takes aim at 30 degrees above horizontal, initial speed 40 m/s. This shot looks simple, but how far will the arrow actually travel?
At first glance, this seems complex - the arrow curves through space while gravity pulls it down and forward motion continues

nodes.title: The Independence Revelation
nodes.objective: Introduce the key insight that horizontal and vertical motions are independent
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has seen the problem setup with archer shooting at 30° with v₀ = 40 m/s, and observed the complex curved trajectory

beat_outline.narration_hint: But here's the beautiful secret: this complex curved motion is actually two simple, independent motions happening simultaneously
We can decompose the initial velocity into horizontal and vertical components using trigonometry
Horizontal component: 40 cos(30°) = 40 × (√3/2) = 20√3 ≈ 34.6 m/s. Vertical component: 40 sin(30°) = 40 × (1/2) = 20 m/s

nodes.question_hint: If the initial speed is 50 m/s at 30°, what is the horizontal component of velocity? (Give your answer to 1 decimal place)
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Let's review trigonometry in projectile motion - which trig function gives us the horizontal component?

wrong_path_acts.title: Trigonometry Review
wrong_path_acts.objective: Clarify which trigonometric function to use for horizontal vs vertical components

beat_outline.narration_hint: Remember: cosine gives us the adjacent side (horizontal), sine gives us the opposite side (vertical)
For horizontal component: velocity × cos(angle). For vertical component: velocity × sin(angle)

nodes.title: Horizontal Motion Story
nodes.objective: Establish that horizontal motion is uniform with constant velocity
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has learned velocity components: horizontal = 34.6 m/s, vertical = 20 m/s, and understands they are independent

beat_outline.narration_hint: Let's focus on horizontal motion first. No forces act horizontally on the arrow during flight
This means horizontal velocity stays constant at 34.6 m/s throughout the entire flight
Horizontal position increases linearly: x = v_x × t = 34.6t

nodes.title: Vertical Motion Story
nodes.objective: Show that vertical motion follows standard gravity pattern with symmetric rise and fall
nodes.context_from_previous: Student understands horizontal motion is uniform at 34.6 m/s and has seen the linear x vs t relationship

beat_outline.narration_hint: Now for vertical motion - here gravity constantly pulls downward at 9.8 m/s²
The vertical velocity starts at 20 m/s upward, decreases due to gravity, becomes zero at the peak, then increases downward
Vertical position follows: y = v₀y×t - ½gt² = 20t - 4.9t²
Notice the beautiful symmetry - the upward journey mirrors the downward journey

nodes.title: Finding Flight Time
nodes.objective: Calculate the total time the arrow stays in the air using the vertical motion equation
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has seen both motion components: horizontal uniform motion and vertical motion with gravity creating symmetric parabolic path y = 20t - 4.9t²

beat_outline.narration_hint: To find the range, we need the total flight time. When does the arrow return to ground level?
Set y = 0 in our equation: 0 = 20t - 4.9t²
Factor out t: 0 = t(20 - 4.9t), giving us t = 0 (launch) or t = 20/4.9 ≈ 4.08 seconds

nodes.question_hint: If an arrow is shot straight up with initial vertical velocity 25 m/s, how long until it returns to the starting height?
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Remember that for symmetric projectile motion, we can use the fact that vertical velocity becomes zero at the peak

wrong_path_acts.title: Vertical Motion Symmetry
wrong_path_acts.objective: Reinforce understanding of symmetric projectile motion timing
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student needs clarification on projectile motion timing

beat_outline.narration_hint: For vertical motion under gravity, the time to reach maximum height equals the time to fall back down
Total flight time is always 2 × (time to peak) = 2 × (v₀y/g)

nodes.title: Calculating the Range
nodes.objective: Combine horizontal motion with flight time to find the final answer
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has calculated flight time as 4.08 seconds using vertical motion equation and understands horizontal velocity is constant at 34.6 m/s

beat_outline.narration_hint: Now we multiply: horizontal speed times total flight time gives us the range
Range = vₓ × t = 34.6 × 4.08 = 141.4 meters
Watch as our arrow lands exactly at this calculated distance

nodes.title: The Complete Picture
nodes.objective: Show the beautiful synthesis and provide satisfying visual payoff
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has calculated the range as 141.4 meters and seen the arrow land at this exact distance

beat_outline.narration_hint: And there's the complete story - two simple motions creating one elegant arc
This distance makes sense - about 1.5 football fields for a skilled archer's shot
The general formula emerges naturally: Range = (v₀² sin(2θ))/g

nodes.question_hint: What would happen to the range if we doubled the initial speed to 80 m/s (keeping the same angle)?
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Think about how range depends on initial speed - look at the range formula R = (v₀² sin(2θ))/g

wrong_path_acts.title: Range Formula Analysis
wrong_path_acts.objective: Help student understand quadratic dependence of range on initial speed
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student needs help understanding how range scales with initial speed

beat_outline.narration_hint: The range formula shows range is proportional to v₀² - the initial speed squared
So doubling the speed (×2) makes the range four times larger (×2²)
